reasoning: A has the sharpest epistemics: it explicitly catches the substitution trap on (b) (answering unconditional P(STEM), not P(STEM|STEM-or-business)), separates undergraduate from graduate degrees, names the load-bearing inputs in (a) with a clean sensitivity table, and even flags and rejects potential circularity in its outside-view check. B is the most empirically grounded (real BLS OEWS Chicago-metro figure and the Crist|Kolder 2025 report with the economics-under-liberal-arts nuance), but it leaves an internal contradiction unreconciled: it cites a BLS metro count of 360 for the broader repairer/tuner category yet concludes 250-450 metro piano tuners, a subset exceeding its own cited superset. C is a competent two-path Fermi with appropriate ranges and good undergrad-vs-highest-degree handling, but its BLS cross-check is hand-wavy and it surfaces fewer cognitive traps than A. All three correctly identify business/econ as the larger CEO category and converge on ~30-33% STEM, which is well-calibrated.
